FYI: I only suggested a function because it might make it a little clearer.  The real issue is the use of ">>" to redirect to a command - ash does not support that, thus replacing it with pipes a simple way around it.  However, you can't directly send stderr to a pipe, which is why the example I gave before wrapped things in parentheses and used two different pipes

not sure - depends how it is to be used.  Appears that .profile is only read by a "login" shell, but any environment variables exported there will be propagated to sub-shells.  If that's how it's used, then .profile makes sense.  OTOH, cron jobs would have to explicitly "source" env.sh as cron does not execute .profile - this shouldn't matter in the ONAP context/environment.
definitely should be double quotes around $@
it seems like only hard coded the function name way can make sure to print out the function name.

i think we are ok to remove this line. In k8s, we used base.conf to "source" env variables. In standalone docker, we used other env-file for all env variables. In env.sh, it just deals with POLICY_HOME env var only.

I am concerned that is going to break some form of operation, there was an usage at some point for it, but can't remember, not sure if it is while doing development with the docker image as in tutorials, or something else.   In any case, if at all possible, I would prefer to keep same functionality as it was originally with the login shell configured to work with the system.

As a note, drools pdp supports not only environment files passed in but also config files that will be read in .conf files.

adding JVM_OPTIONS=-verbose:gc -XX:+PrintGC in envfile. it seems to work.

hmm.  did you check "ps" to see the whole command line (if it lets you)?  Seems to be that double quotes are needed around ${JVM_OPTIONS}.  As an alternative, could eliminate JVM_OPTS altogether and just use JVM_OPTIONS on the java command line below.

I don't think line 58 is needed; in fact, not sure it has any effect in "ash".  (Ran a short test with ash to verify.)
Without this, the variable, $systemProperties, will contain embedded newlines between each property.  However, the shell will replace them with spaces on the java command line below, provided $systemProperties is not quoted.  (If it WERE quoted, then it would retain the newlines and treat it as a single argument, which we don't want anyway.)
yes, this appears to work, based on a quick test

i think both should all work. the msg in echo statement is leading some kind of ambiguous.

$name -> is a feature intended for uninstall
$aFeature -> is a feature in /opt/app/policy/feature

I would prefer just adding more msg in echo statement.

no, they will not both work.  the way this is written, if there were four features in "allFeatures", then it is guaranteed to execute line 1170 three times for each $name, even if $name matches one of those features.  The original code (and the code with the flag), on the other hand, would have never executed line 1170 unless $name didn't match.

the bottom line: line 1170 should not appear within the inner loop

here is the example:
/tmp/policy-install # ls /opt/app/policy/features/
distributed-locking  healthcheck  lifecycle

allFeatures would come with three values: distributed-locking  healthcheck  lifecycle
line 1165 - 1172 is the loop for allFeatures
so aFeauture would have distributed-locking, healthcheck and lifecycle three loops
if executing: "features uninstall healthcheck lifecycle"
$@=healthcheck lifecycle
so only line 1168 would do : uninstallFeature healthcheck then lifecycle

/tmp/policy-install # features uninstall healthcheck lifecycle
intend to uninstall feature: healthcheck, not matched with feature: distributed-locking
intend to uninstall feature: healthcheck, not matched with feature: lifecycle
intend to uninstall feature: lifecycle, not matched with feature: distributed-locking
intend to uninstall feature: lifecycle, not matched with feature: healthcheck
/tmp/policy-install # ls /opt/app/policy/features/
distributed-locking

/tmp/policy-install # features status
name                 version         status
----                 -------         ------
distributed-locking  1.8.0-SNAPSHOT  disabled

I even think can remove line 1169 else portion - that does not really do anything.

If I ran the flag version.

features uninstall healthcheck aa

that won't echo "feature aa not found"
since flag has been set true due to the existing feature: healthcheck

The example you provided demonstrates the problem: since healthcheck is a valid feature, it should never have reported:

    intend to uninstall feature: healthcheck, not matched with feature: distributed-locking
    intend to uninstall feature: healthcheck, not matched with feature: lifecycle

It should only report features that are not matched at all during the inner loop.  So it should not report healthcheck or lifecycle at all, because those are matched by one of the features in the inner loop, while it should report that "aa" was unmatched
